registry.description: Memory is the inner capacity to hold the past present — to keep what has happened in living connection with the present so that it shapes how one lives. The Hebrew vocabulary here (zikaron) is primarily the memorial, the act of commemoration. Memory in Scripture is fundamentally theological: Israel is called again and again to remember what God has done. To forget is not merely an intellectual failure but a spiritual one: the person who forgets what God has done loses the foundation of their trust in what he will do.

perfect ἐμνημόνευκα [Refs 5th c.BC+]:—passive, future μνημονευθήσομαι [Refs 5th c.BC+]; also μνημονεύσομαι in passive sense (see. infr. B): aorist ἐμνημονεύθην[Refs 5th c.BC+]:—middle, aorist ἐμνημονευσάμην [Refs 2nd c.AD+]: (μνήμων):— call to mind, remember, think of, with accusative, [Refs 5th c.BC+]: with infinitive, remember to do, [Refs 5th c.BC+]; μ. ὅτι.. [Refs 5th c.BC+]; εἰ.. [Refs 4th c.BC+]; ὡς.. [LXX]
__2 absolutely, μ. γὰρ καλῶς [Refs 5th c.BC+]; opposed to ἀναμιμνήσκεσθαι, as animal instinct from human faculty, [Refs 4th c.BC+]
__II call to another's mind, mention, say, with accusative, ἀληθῆ -εύεις [Refs 5th c.BC+]; μ. τινί τινος make mention of a thing to another, [Refs 3rd c.BC+]: with genitive, make mention of, τοῦ ἔθνους ἐν ταῖς ἱστορίαις J.[same place],[LXX+1st c.AD+]
__III serve as μνήμων [Refs 5th c.BC+]; μ. ἁγναῖς θεαῖς [Refs]
__B passive, to be remembered, had in memory, μνημονεύσεται χάρις [Refs 5th c.BC+]; τὰ ἐκ τοῦ πρὶν χρόνου μνημονευόμενα [Refs 5th c.BC+]: with infinitive, ἐμνημονεύετο γενέσθαι [Refs 5th c.BC+]: with participle, πόλεμος.. εὖ πολεμηθεὶς μ. [Refs 5th c.BC+]
